speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three-- tape six.

speaker: interviewee
text: So initially, they were trying to make sense of this group. And of course-- so they brought in experts and what have you-- psychiatrists, psychologist. And again, they couldn't make head or tails of these wild kids that were there. But, you know, little by little, you know, the dust was settling. And this one time, they-- apparently there was somebody that really knew what they were doing. And so this one person-- and again, I don't remember his name. We were guarded one evening. And there's just sort of somebody special here that want to speak to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And apparently, this man wouldn't take any nonsense. You know? And he knew how to deal with adolescents and kids. He was apparently an expert in the field. So we were guarding around here, and he started talking. And he-- he-- his-- his words were drowned, because nobody was listening. So he got-- he got mad-- took his jacket off and was rolling up his sleeves. You know, he was gonna show these kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: As he was rolling up his sleeves, we saw his Auschwitz number. So there was dead silence. And he looks up everybody, and he sees this dead silence. And he can't figure it out. And then he starts off by saying [NON-ENGLISH]. And that was it. And he broke down. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He started crying. And most of us started crying with him. And we became human that-- that-- that-- that evening. And after that, they didn't seem to have any problems. He didn't say anything else; put his jacket back on; walked away. And I don't know to this day what his message was. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It just didn't happen. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That must have been-- oh, several months after the liberation. And this was the beginning of the process of becoming human again. We-- in my own case, I was very, very fortunate. There was a professor at the Sorbonne-- Manfred Reingwitz-- who sort of took a liking to me. And I don't know very much about his history, because I was so wrapped up in my own studies. He just showered me with work. You know, the 12 hours that I used to put in the munitions factory I was putting in catching up in my schooling. And he had one conversation with me. And in that conversation he said, you have to become a mensch, and you got to go back to school. And you got to forget those years in the camps. And you got to forget-- the word Holocaust didn't exist then. You got to forget.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to him, are you crazy? You want me to forget everything that was dear to me, everything that I loved in life? They took everything away from me. You just want me to forget and forgive?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, no. You can never forget what you've seen. But you've got to find a way of putting that aside. Let it be there. Bring it out now and then. But let it be there. Concentrate on being a little boy again. Be a teenager. Go to school. Make a life for yourself. Get married, have children.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what I said to him? You're crazy-- totally rebelled-- wouldn't listen to him. But he kept on me until the other shoe dropped, so to speak. And I started doing. I got involved in the language, and-- and-- and so on and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: And again, you know, I didn't have an opportunity really to-- because he ultimately died before I had a chance to say-- well, I did say, you know, because we corresponded. You know, and then I lost-- he moved somewhere. And I-- and then I couldn't find him. But if he had lived around today, as-- you know, thank you, Mr. Manfred. I used to call him Mr. Manfred-- not Mr. Reingwitz. He was a wonderful human being. I have pictures of him with me. Any time that you see these pictures that I have from after the liberation, he's always somewhere in the background-- looking after me. And so there you have it in a nutshell. This-- there were all kinds of opportunities for us in France. But I remember thinking there were so many-- so many memories, or every-- everywhere you turn, it was Europe. And so most of us decided to turn our back on Europe and go and make a new life for yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we looked where-- where is there an open door? We picked two places-- Canada and Australia. Canada was the number one. Some were talking about Australia, for the simple reason that if ever there's another war, and if you're in Australia, you'll be completely sheltered.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, we didn't-- the atomic bomb was already dropped. So we said-- we thought somebody-- guys were saying, in Australia it won't touch you. If there's gonna be another war, it's probably gonna be in-- in Europe. And you'll be safe there. It took three months to-- to get to Australia-- no jet travel yet, you know, in the '40s-- in the latter part of the '40s.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you come to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came end of 1949, almost the beginning of 1950-- when I arrived in Canada. I remember when we docked in-- in Halifax, and we took the-- a train. And I looked at the-- the planes, and-- and the farms, and the distances. And scarcely you would see a farmhouse, and-- and I-- even then I thought to myself, my God, so much space here. Look how many people could have come in here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, later, of course, I found that there was no room for us in here in-- in Canada. When-- when-- when 500 children in that boat tried to-- to land in Canada and-- and some of the leadership in the Jewish communities went to see Mackenzie King and-- and a fellow by the name of Blair, who was the minister of immigration-- and when Mackenzie asked Blair how many will you let in? How many can we let in? And he said, none is too many. And then, of course, you know Abella wrote the book Did you experience any anti-Semitism in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't know. Coming from Poland and coming through the Holocaust, I don't know. Maybe I wasn't as sensitive. But I have, you know, worked. I was in the-- the hotel business. And I was exposed to gentiles left and right. I must say that at the beginning, I thought nobody knows that I'm Jewish. And I didn't volunteer that I am Jewish. But that's sort of-- you're kidding yourself, because people know that you're Jewish. But anyway, I felt that I better-- no sense, you know, shouting about it. But when my son and daughter were born-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: When were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning of the '60s. And when my daughter came home and-- and start asking what she can take to school for show and tell, to tell them about Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, I-- I was almost panic stricken. Because I said, you-- you're going to talk about that? She said, yes. Daddy, kids would be very interested.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was worried sick, because I thought oh, she's gonna be exposed that she's Jewish. What if somebody beats her up because of it? Because you know, I applied my experience in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- Were there any other ways that your war experience affected the way you raised your children or experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this-- this was one experience, you know, that I wanted to shelter them. And so I would say that I learned a lot from my kids, because they were born in this country, and they didn't-- they didn't have the baggage that I had, OK? Although I didn't-- again, I was trying to protect my kids from my nightmare that I went through. I didn't speak to my son about it. My wife was out of town. And we found ourselves in a restaurant one day. And he left something on the plate. He wasn't very hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- instinctively, I looked at this food, and I-- and I said to him, oh, what I wouldn't have given to have this kind of food in the camp. What camp? He was 11 at that time-- 11 or 12.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I start telling him. I was your age when I went through-- and that-- that's why you don't have a grandmother and a grandfather. And I start telling him a little bit. You know, they had to chase us out, because they were turning out the lights and I was still talking to him and telling him some of the things. And that was the first time that I had talked about my experiences.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife would know bit by bit, because I would have nightmares. And then she would ask, what? You were talking about something, this and this. I say, oh, some of these camp years come back. What camp years? And so-- What type of nightmares where you having?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would find myself in a selection. I went to two of these selections. If you saw I didn't talk about it. I don't know if you want me-- do we have time to tell you about? My father, in one of the-- in Skarzysko-- wanted to shield me from one of these selections. And so he bribed a guard. And I escaped one of the selections. I went into the woods, and I was with the partisans there-- with a bunch of Poles. I was out there for some time. I didn't tell you about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But every time they would drink. They didn't trust me. I was a gopher, you know? They didn't give me a gun to shoot or anything like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when-- the one time-- when they would drink, you know, which was fairly often, they would take potshots at me. And once a bullet flew-- almost touched my ear. I thought to myself, if I stay with this group, I'd be gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who the leader of the group was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No idea. It was just a group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing about--

speaker: interviewer
text: --about their activity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were doing all sorts of blowing up trains and what have you. But I didn't stay very long there. I found the same hole in the camp and-- and went back into the camp. I was safer inside than out there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So another selection was happening. There were all-- all sorts of selections. And this time, I was in the camp. And so when I came in front of the Gestapo, and when he motioned me to the left, and my father landed up on the right side. And when I looked around me I saw some elderly and some sick people. And I immediately figured I wouldn't be very long if I stayed in this group. So I run up to the Gestapo man. And I-- I said to him, [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH]. And I pointed to the direction that I came from, rather than the other. He wasn't gonna grant me my wish. You see? So he just hit me over the head and pointed me to, 'Go back there,' he said. And when I got back with my father, I realized that I had made a mistake. So though-- when I-- earlier I said I did all kinds of stupid things that saved my life. That was one of them. I forgot to tell you about it. So-- And this stays with you in the form of nightmares?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When you talk to survivors, sometimes when you're in the car and you stop in front of a railroad crossing, that takes a load off you immediately, because you-- you-- you see the trains go by, and it-- it all floods back. Sometimes a smell of something-- memories come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I do what my-- what Manfred Reingwitz suggested-- put it aside. But now, as we get older, and you go full circle, some of these memories intensify. And it-- it-- it comes back more and more.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was rebuilding my life, it was easier than it is now. It comes back. And it's-- you have to deal with-- with memory. You have to deal with a lot of these things. I still do not have closure. You know, for the longest time when the Iron Curtain was in Russia, I thought that one of my brothers might-- may be still alive. You know, I'm-- I'm really kidding myself. And-- and I-- I plan to go back to Poland and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been back to your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have not been back. My wife is always against that. She says, you're going back to pain. Why do you want that? But deep down, I think I'm looking for closure of some sort-- if there is such a thing. If we can get it-- to-- to deal with-- with this process. It's probably easier, because again, you know, when I had my own children, I began to feel that much more guilt with the episode with my father, because I suddenly became a father and realized what it is like to have your own children. And if somebody was to tell me that I have to go through this with my own kids, God, I wouldn't be able to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lost your parents. You lost your brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. Sometimes I think that there's a purpose to it. And then people talk about 1.5 million children. And when I speak about it to the students and tell 'em what can happen when enough good people, in the face of evil, look the other way-- you know, I speak to them as the child that I was and on behalf of the children that are no more. And when I think of some of the friends that I had immediate in my own circle, and 1.5 million Jewish children that are not-- that-- that weren't able to get married, have children, and do what I have done, and-- and all of a sudden, when I put it in perspective, I say to myself, I have an obligation. I have to speak for those that perished. I have to speak for those that are not here. And I have to tell the world. I have to do what this man once told me when the lights were turning out-- to tell the world. And you know, for the longest time, the world wasn't interested in listening. For years and years, we had to keep it inside. And now--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what is your message now to the world, to the next generation?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the next generation, the greatest fear the people in the camps was that, look. They're killing us. They are annihilating the entire population. Nobody will ever remember that we existed. It'll be wiped off the face of the earth. A spark is being left-- a few survivors. A few of the people from 1,200, now number 6,000-- all of us now-- and Elie Wiesel, I give him credit for giving us the courage to come out and speak. And he said, and I quote him, 'Remember, there is hope in remembering'. That's what I want.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you very much for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for listening to me and-- and-- and sharing the-- the agony that I had to do in speaking to you. I-- I appreciate it, because, again, I-- I-- I realize how important it is that this be-- I-- I have 10 more tapes that-- things that I didn't tell you. Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] Fran, this is my distinct pleasure, and I'm really delighted to be able to present to you my wife, Gloria, who has been married to-- to me since December 21, 1958. And she's been great in-- in putting up with my nightmares and-- and helping me to become a human being again. I'm really grateful to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And over to the other side, my son Howard Steven, my treasure. What can I say about him? He's just a delightful son. And Arlaina, who isn't here, my daughter, who wasn't able to come, my other treasure. And those are my dividends and my victory over Hitler's Holocaust. They're here to continue. And sitting down is Gloria's mom, my mother-in-law, Dorothy Goldenberg, who is here with us. And I'm delighted to present her to you as well. OK. Howie, did you want to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, I'm just-- I wanted to add that I'm glad that this is taking place, that this testimony is happening and is being recorded. It's significant that my only grandmother is here and the only grandmother I've ever known. Unfortunately, I didn't get the opportunity to meet my other grandparents and my uncles. And hopefully this will be a memory for them, which will last for a long, long time to come so that other people will know that this happened, and that it's not just a-- a confabulation-- something someone made up. And essentially, people can learn from this. And esentially, that's what I'd like to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the picture of Arlaina?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. OK. This is my treasure. This is my-- a picture of my eldest brother, Chaim. It was taken in 1937 or '38-- [BACKROUND NOISE] an officer in the army. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] OK. This is a picture of us kids walking out of the concentration camp towards the SS barracks. Underneath it says [NON-ENGLISH]-- a few of the stronger ones that have-- that had been liberated. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] And here we're looking at a picture after-- shortly after the liberation. We're being examined by a nurse and a doctor, getting medical treatment, which was a novelty in-- after so many years. On the right of the picture is Salek Finkelstein. He's now in New York. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Well, this is a picture of my friend [? Lulek Lau ?]. [? Lulek Lau ?], as you see it here, is leaving France for Palestine, for the state of Israel. I'm happy to report to you that he is now the Chief Rabbi of Israel with eight kids. Nice going, [? Lulek ?]! [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] OK. This picture was taken outside of the camp of Buchenwald. In the background, you see the SS barracks. And these uniforms that you see were made to-- for us by a tailor. We didn't have any clothes-- or they were all ragged looking. So they took Nazi uniforms and cut down to-- to fit us-- like, all short pants as you see. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] OK. On the far right is Salek Finkelstein-- lives in New York. Next to him is Abraham Chopnik, also in New York. Next to Abraham is myself. And in the front-- in the front row, next to Salek is [? Lulek Lau ?], the Chief Rabbi of Israel that I spoke about. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Well, this is a-- this is a group of-- of us. I'm right in the middle there. And in front there's [NON-ENGLISH], who is in Australia. [NON-ENGLISH], Toronto; [NON-ENGLISH], in-- in-- where is he-- San Francisco; [NON-ENGLISH], in Paris, France-- scattered all over the world. Oh, how close we were then. We never thought that we would ever separate. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This was taken at Ferriere-- Chateau Ferriere. Rothschilds owned that chateau. And we spent a month in there in the summer-- me in the middle there with arms folded. Next to me is professor Manfred Reingwitz, who helped me to become human again.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in 1946. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This picture was taken in 1947. Here I am standing in uniform-- in the-- the Buchenwald uniform. We were picked to put a-- a wreath on the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier under the Arch of the Triumph in Paris, France.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The second one. There's two-- you see the two fellows standing in uniform? I'm the second one-- the second from left. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Now this picture was taken with Elie Wiesel when he was here in Vancouver, in 1989. Look at the-- the expression on his face, because I've shown him my album. And he is providing commentary for some of the pictures. Dr. Krell is standing right beside me there. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture was taken in 1991 with my liberator, Dr. Leon Bass, who was here in Vancouver. And we've become very good friends. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mid-80s. OK. Now this is a picture of my lovely daughter, Arlaina Joy. It was taken in the mid '80s when she graduated from the University of British Columbia. Just sorry that she couldn't be with us here today. Then again, Fran, as I told you before, both my children are-- are my-- my treasure-- everything. They mean an awful lot to me.
